en.extract:
The southern scrub robin (Drymodes brunneopygia) is a species of bird in the family Petroicidae. It is endemic to Australia, where it occurs in mallee and heathland in the semi-arid southern parts of the continent. Its range  includes several disjoint regions from central New South Wales through western Victoria and southern South Australia, through to the southwestern area of Western Australia.


Taxonomy
The southern scrub robin was formally described in 1841 by the English ornithologist John Gould under the current binomial name Drymodes brunneopygia. Gould noted that the bird was found near the Murray River in South Australia. The specific epithet combines the Modern Latin brunneus, meaning "brown", with the Ancient Greek -pugios, meaning "-rumped". The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
It is a relatively dull and large robin, adults being around 19–20 centimetres (7.5–7.9 in) in length, of which around a third is the tail feathers. Most of the plumage is grey, except for a dullish red tail and patterned black-and-white wings. The legs are unusually long for a passerine, and are frequently used to hop through the dense heathland that forms the bird's habitat, where it searches for insects and other small invertebrates.


Behaviour


Breeding
The birds breed between July and December. The nest is built of twigs on the ground and is lined with twigs, grass and bark. Unusually for a passerine, the southern scrub robin lays only a single egg. It is grey-green in colour for camouflage amongst the sclerophyllous flora that forms its habitat. The egg is incubated for sixteen days. The young leave the nest after 10–13 days but continue to be fed by both parents.


Status
The species is listed under the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species as a species of 'Least concern'.  In New South Wales it is listed as Vulnerable under the Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016.


References

The '''southern scrub robin''' ('''''Drymodes brunneopygia''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Petroicidae]]. It is [[endemism|endemic]] to [[Australia]], where it occurs in [[mallee (habit)|mallee]] and [[heathland]] in the semi-arid southern parts of the continent.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=CqBCEQAAQBAJ&pg=PT784 |page=784 |title=Finding Australian Birds: A Field Guide to Birding Locations |author1=Tim Dolby |author2=Rohan Clarke |publisher=Csiro Publishing |year=2025 |isbn=978-1-4863-1522-2 }}</ref> Its range  includes several disjoint regions from central [[New South Wales]] through western Victoria and southern South Australia, through to the southwestern area of Western Australia.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021"/>

==Taxonomy==
The southern scrub robin was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1841 by the English ornithologist [[John Gould]] under the current [[binomial name]] ''Drymodes brunneopygia''. Gould noted that the bird was found near the [[Murray River]] in [[South Australia]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1840 | title=''Drymodes brunneopygia'' | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=8 | issue=95  | pages=170–171 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30571609 }} Although the volume has the year 1840 on the title page, the article did not appear until 1841. For the publication date see: {{ cite journal | last=Sclater | first=P.L. | author-link=Philip Sclater | year=1893 | title=List of the dates of delivery of the sheets of the 'Proceedings' of the Zoological Society of London, from the commencement in 1830 to 1859 inclusive | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | volume=<!--not specified on title page--> | pages=436–440 [437] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35992701 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Paynter | editor2-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1964 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=10 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=29 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14486218 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Dickinson | editor1-first=E.C. | editor1-link=Edward C. Dickinson  | editor2-last=Christidis | editor2-first=L. | editor2-link=Leslie Christidis | year=2014 | title=The Howard & Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World | volume=2: Passerines | edition=4th | location=Eastbourne, UK | publisher=Aves Press | isbn=978-0-9568611-2-2 | page=258 }}</ref> The specific epithet combines the [[Modern Latin]] {{Lang|la|brunneus}}, meaning "brown", with the [[Ancient Greek]] {{Lang|grc-Latn|-pugios}}, meaning "-rumped".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=79 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n79/mode/1up }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=July 2023 | title=Australasian robins, rockfowl, rockjumpers, Rail-babbler | work=IOC World Bird List Version 13.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/au_robins/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=26 December 2023 }}</ref>

==Description==
It is a relatively dull and large robin, adults being around {{convert|19–20|cm|in|1}} in length, of which around a third is the tail feathers. Most of the plumage is grey,<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kZqJAAAAQBAJ&pg=PA497 |page=497 |title=A Dictionary of Birds |author1=Bruce Campbell |author2=Elizabeth Lack |publisher=A & C Black |year=2013 |isbn=978-1-4081-3839-7 }}</ref> except for a dullish red tail and patterned black-and-white wings. The legs are unusually long for a passerine, and are frequently used to hop through the dense heathland that forms the bird's habitat, where it searches for [[insect]]s and other small invertebrates.

==Behaviour==
===Breeding===
The birds breed between July and December. The nest is built of twigs on the ground and is lined with twigs, grass and bark. Unusually for a [[passerine]], the southern scrub robin lays only a single egg. It is grey-green in colour for camouflage amongst the [[Sclerophyll|sclerophyllous]] flora that forms its habitat. The egg is incubated for sixteen days. The young leave the nest after 10–13 days but continue to be fed by both parents.<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last=Boles | first=W.E. | year=2007 | chapter=Family Petroicidae (Australasian robins) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=12: Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-96553-42-2 | pages=438–489 [468] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0012unse/page/468/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref>

==Status==
The species is listed under the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN Red List of Threatened Species]] as a species of 'Least concern'.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021"/>  In [[New South Wales]] it is listed as ''Vulnerable'' under the [[Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016 (NSW)|Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016]].<ref name="OEH">{{cite web |url=https://threatenedspecies.bionet.nsw.gov.au/profile?id=10252 |title=Southern Scrub-robin - profile |publisher=NSW Office of Environment & Heritage | access-date = 16 December 2023}}</ref>
